author: mann1x
message:
Preserve user-initiated captures in UndoCapture on session lock

UndoCapture fires on SessionLock and on power-state changes. Its intent
is to rewind a capture that landed within CaptureLatency (3 s) of those
events, on the assumption that the OS may have transiently mutated
window state as part of the lock/sleep transition and the capture
should not be the restore target. It rewinds the snapshot time pointer
to the prior capture.

This is too aggressive for one common user pattern: minimize a window,
then press Win+L within 2-3 s. The user's minimize generates a capture
that UndoCapture then rolls back. On unlock the batch restore reads the
pre-minimize record and the window comes back un-minimized — wrong.

Reproduced today: minimize Netflix, lock, unlock → Netflix appeared in
the foreground. Event log:
  21:50:32 Session closing: reason SessionLock
  21:50:32 undo capture of <displayKey> at 21:50:29
  21:54:29 Start restoring window layout back to 21:50:27
            (the pre-minimize capture)

Fix: PW already tracks whether the most recent capture cycle was
triggered by user activity. userMove is set to true by WinEventProc on
events for known windows (EVENT_OBJECT_CREATE, EVENT_SYSTEM_MINIMIZEEND/
MINIMIZESTART/MOVESIZEEND on a window in monitorApplications or in
allUserMoveWindows). The CaptureApplicationsOnCurrentDisplays loop
snapshots userMove into userMovePrev right before resetting it
(line 777), so userMovePrev reflects "the last completed capture was
user-initiated."

UndoCapture now early-returns when userMovePrev is true, preserving
the user-intent state change. OS-only transients (no associated user
WinEvent on a tracked window) leave userMovePrev false and continue to
be eligible for rollback as before.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Move WinEvent fast filters outside captureLock so Alt+Tab survives batch restore

PR #456 added a class-name filter for the Alt+Tab switcher / Task View
overlays (Task Switching, MultitaskingViewFrame, XamlExplorerHostIslandWindow),
but the check lives *inside* WinEventProcCore — which runs under captureLock.
During a batch restore (display change recovery), captureLock is held for
several seconds while the worker thread processes 5-pass placement updates
on every window. Every WinEvent that arrives during that window — including
the switcher's own EVENT_OBJECT_CREATE — has to wait for the lock before
the filter can short-circuit. Explorer's queued message responses pile up
behind the lock, and the Alt+Tab switcher overlay can take ~1s to render
when the user presses Alt+Tab mid-restore.

Observed: monitor flap at 21:38:47-49 → session unlock + restore at
21:38:52-21:39:00 → Alt+Tab pressed during the restore → switcher took
~1s to appear (window list visibly delayed).

Fix: move the cheap pre-lock filters above lock(captureLock). None of
them touch shared state:

  - hwnd == IntPtr.Zero
  - non-window object filter (idObject != 0 for non-CREATE events)
  - IsTransientShellWindow class-name check (GetClassName is kernel-only,
    never blocks)

These checks now run on the STA hook thread *without* blocking on the
restore worker. The Alt+Tab switcher's WinEvents return in microseconds
even while a batch restore is in flight. Explorer stays responsive, the
switcher renders immediately on Alt+Tab.

The same early-returns inside WinEventProcCore are kept for
defense-in-depth in case the function ever gains another caller. They are
now redundant for the WinEventProc path but cost nothing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Skip MoveWindow when SetWindowPlacement already positioned the window

After a display change, every Alt+Tab to a previously-minimized window
takes the "restore minimized window" branch in ActivateWindow, which
calls SetWindowPlacement followed unconditionally by MoveWindow. The
second call is a redundant synchronous round-trip when SetWindowPlacement
already produced the desired geometry — which is the common case now
that WindowPlacement.NormalPosition is stored correctly (see 2331dae,
22779a5).

The cost shows up on heavy UI-thread targets: UWP apps (Netflix), browsers
re-establishing GPU compositors after the display flap. SetWindowPlacement
itself blocks until the target processes the placement message;
MoveWindow blocks again for another paint cycle. On Netflix during the
first Alt+Tab after a monitor disconnect/reconnect cycle this adds ~1s
of perceived lag before the window appears.

Fix: after SetWindowPlacement, query GetWindowRect; only call MoveWindow
when the actual rect doesn't match target_rect. The MoveWindow path is
preserved for the snap-state correction cases that motivated it — it just
no longer fires when it would be a no-op.

Same pattern as the existing screenPosition.Equals(target_rect) short-circuit
at line ~2040; this is the symmetrical check after the placement call
rather than before.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Also patch WindowPlacement.ShowCmd to Normal when substituting NormalPosition

Live-test follow-up: with only the previous commit's NormalPosition
substitution, ActivateWindow still bailed with "no qualified position
data to restore minimized window" because its check is an OR:

    if (prevDisplayMetrics.WindowPlacement.ShowCmd == ShowMinimized
       || prevDisplayMetrics.WindowPlacement.ShowCmd == Minimize
       || target_rect.Left <= -25600)
        return; // bail

The previous commit fixed the third clause (target_rect.Left). The
first two still triggered because the captured WindowPlacement.ShowCmd
was ShowMinimized.

Fix: when substituting NormalPosition for ScreenPosition at capture
time, also set WindowPlacement.ShowCmd = Normal. The stored metrics
are now internally consistent: "we have a valid normal-state rect, and
the show-cmd matches." ActivateWindow no longer bails, and
SetWindowPlacement during restore correctly unminimizes the window
into NormalPosition rather than re-minimizing it.

The separate IsMinimized=true field is still set, so display-change
restore paths that legitimately want to re-minimize a window can still
do so via that signal.

Verified on Notepad: previously, a freshly-opened minimized Notepad
unminimize-via-Alt+Tab logged "no qualified position" and PW gave up;
with this patch the captured ShowCmd is Normal, the bail-out does not
fire, and PW restores the window to its NormalPosition rect.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Capture minimized windows with their NormalPosition, not the parking rect

When a window is minimized, User32.GetWindowRect returns the off-screen
parking position (typically -32000,-32000). IsWindowMoved was recording
that as ScreenPosition, polluting the per-window history. Later, when
the user unminimized the window (e.g. via Alt+Tab), ActivateWindow read
back the saved ScreenPosition, found target_rect.Left <= -25600, logged
"no qualified position data to restore minimized window <title>" and
returned without restoring. The captured data was useless.

GetWindowPlacement already returns NormalPosition - the rectangle the
window would have when restored to normal state - and the call was
already being made on the same line. Use NormalPosition as
ScreenPosition when the window is currently minimized AND NormalPosition
is on-screen (left > -25600). This eliminates the polluted records,
makes subsequent restore attempts succeed, and removes the wasted CPU /
event-log noise from the "no qualified position" path.

Safety guard: if NormalPosition is also off-screen (rare edge case,
e.g. a window minimized on a now-disconnected monitor), keep the old
behavior so existing IsOffScreen / CenterWindow paths still trigger.

Observed before the fix in today's event log: multiple
"no qualified position data to restore minimized window" entries for
Telegram, Obsidian, command prompts and other unminimize-via-Alt+Tab
events on this machine.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Skip Alt+Tab switcher and Task View overlays in WinEventProc

The Win32 Alt+Tab switcher window has class "Task Switching" (classic),
"MultitaskingViewFrame" (Task View), or "XamlExplorerHostIslandWindow"
(Win10/11 newer overlay). These windows appear briefly on every Alt+Tab
and are destroyed within a few hundred ms. Without a filter PW:

  1. Receives EVENT_OBJECT_CREATE  -> StartCaptureTimer
  2. Capture timer fires (after 250ms)  -> CaptureWindow under captureLock
     adds the switcher to monitorApplications
  3. Switcher closes  -> EVENT_OBJECT_DESTROY  -> re-acquires captureLock
     for the full cleanup loop, finds the recent capture, logs
     "discard capture when closing window Task Switching" to the
     Windows Application event log (a synchronous RPC to the Event Log
     Service)

This noise was visible in today's event log: four "discard capture when
closing window Task Switching" entries from normal Alt+Tab use. Each
cycle through the path takes captureLock on the STA hook thread,
contending with concurrent capture/restore operations and adding to the
sluggishness users see when cycling Alt+Tab with PW running.

Fix: filter these three known transient shell UI classes at the very
top of WinEventProcCore. Skip all events except DESTROY -- DESTROY is
still allowed through so any state recorded before this filter existed
can be cleaned up on first encounter.

GetClassName is a fast kernel-only query (no IPC, never blocks on the
target's UI thread), so the filter cost is negligible.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Fix Alt+Tab lag from synchronous WM_GETTEXT and VirtualDesktop COM probe

WinEventProcCore runs on the STA hook thread under captureLock for every
WinEvent. Two calls in the EVENT_SYSTEM_FOREGROUND path stalled the
callback when the user cycled Alt+Tab through windows whose UI thread
was momentarily busy (browsers doing GPU work, etc.):

1. GetWindowTitle -> User32.GetWindowText sends WM_GETTEXT cross-process
   and blocks on the target's UI thread (Vista+ aborts after ~5s, but
   smaller stalls still add up). The cache was populated only by the
   capture/restore paths, so every freshly-tabbed window hit the sync
   path on its first foreground event.

2. VirtualDesktop.GetWindowDesktopId is a COM call that can stall on a
   busy desktop manager. It fires on every foreground change, including
   every Alt+Tab cycle, even though the active virtual desktop cannot
   change while the user is mid-cycling.

Combined with the WinEvent re-entrancy guard added in 9ec288e (which
drops nested callbacks while the STA is blocked), this manifested as
sluggish Alt+Tab cycling and occasional "stuck" cycles where the
switcher visibly froze until the user released Alt or clicked.

Fix:
- Use InternalGetWindowText (undocumented but stable since NT 3.51,
  used by Explorer's own Alt+Tab switcher, Task Manager, Spy++). It
  reads the title from win32k cache without sending a message, so it
  never blocks on the target's UI thread.
- Always populate windowTitle[hwnd] from GetWindowTitle, including the
  empty-string result, so re-tabbing the same hwnd never re-queries.
  The cache is already cleared in EVENT_OBJECT_DESTROY, so stale entries
  cannot leak.
- Skip VirtualDesktop.GetWindowDesktopId in EVENT_SYSTEM_FOREGROUND
  while alt_key_pressed. The foregroundTimer fires after Alt release
  and will probe the desktop then if anything actually changed.

Live-validated on master + this fix: previously-laggy Alt+Tab cycling
is now responsive even with many windows including Brave, VS Code,
Explorer, and other heavy clients.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: Kang Yu
message: exclude taskbar window from deferred msg queue, as taskbar must be moved first for other windows to position correctly

author: mann1x
message:
Fix Brave/PW hang on monitor standby resume from sync SendMessage calls

Four call sites in the restore loop used User32.SendMessage (no timeout)
to send WM_SYSCOMMAND/SC_MINIMIZE and WM_COMMAND/SC_TOGGLE_TASKBAR_LOCK
to target windows. SendMessage blocks until the target's UI thread acks,
with no escape hatch. After GPU/compositor wake-up from monitor standby,
Brave's UI thread is busy re-establishing GPU contexts. With 5 restore
passes over many windows, the cumulative sync messages monopolize Brave's
UI thread for tens of seconds: Brave windows go gray, Brave's tray menu
won't open, and PersistentWindows' own restore worker is stuck.

Killing PersistentWindows immediately recovers Brave (the user's
observation), confirming PW's sync messaging as the root cause.

Fix: replace the 4 raw SendMessage calls with SendMessageTimeout using
SMTO_ABORTIFHUNG and a 200ms timeout (500ms for the taskbar). On timeout,
the window is added to slowResponseWindows so subsequent passes within
the same restore cycle skip the same call, and the (msg, wParam) pair is
queued in deferredCommands. In restoreFinishedTimer, before clearing
state, RetryDeferredCommands() makes one final attempt with a longer
1500ms timeout per command.

This preserves issue #276 intent (slow-but-eventually-responsive windows
still get their command via the deferred retry) while preventing the
restore loop from blocking indefinitely on a single slow target. The
//continue; guard at the IsHungAppWindow check is left untouched.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Skip inheriting off-screen positions from killed windows

When a new window inherits position from a killed window, validate
that the inherited ScreenPosition is actually on a monitor before
restoring. Prevents windows from being placed at bogus off-screen
coordinates inherited from dead windows.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Improve RestoreAllParked to detect all off-screen windows

Use IsRectOffScreen (MonitorFromPoint) instead of only checking for
the -32000 park position. Windows can end up at arbitrary off-screen
coordinates (e.g., from inheriting bogus positions of killed windows).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Fix CPU spinning from re-entrant WinEvent callbacks on STA thread

The WinEventProc callback runs on the main STA thread. When it blocks
on captureLock (held by a timer threadpool thread), the CLR pumps COM
messages while waiting. This delivers more WinEvent callbacks on the
same thread, each also blocking on captureLock. The cascading lock
contention causes deep stack growth and 100%+ CPU usage.

Fix: add a re-entrancy guard flag to skip nested WinEvent callbacks.
These events will be picked up by the next capture timer cycle anyway.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Fix SetWindowPlacement restoring normal windows to wrong monitor

SetWindowPlacement uses workspace-relative NormalPosition coordinates
which can place windows on the wrong monitor in multi-monitor setups.
Previously this was masked because EqualPlacement rarely detected
changes (due to reflection-based RECT.Equals), so NeedUpdateWindowPlacement
was rarely true and SetWindowPlacement was rarely called.

After adding explicit RECT.Equals, position changes are detected
correctly, triggering SetWindowPlacement for normal windows where
MoveWindow with absolute ScreenPosition coordinates is sufficient.

Fix: only call SetWindowPlacement for minimized/maximized/fullscreen
windows where it's needed to restore the correct window state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Fix windows not restoring to secondary monitor after display change

The previous restoringFromMem fix rejected minimize captures entirely,
which also discarded the pre-minimize position data (including the
correct monitor). The OS moves windows to the primary monitor before
minimizing them during display changes, and that move was still being
captured, overwriting the secondary monitor position.

Fix: let the minimize-start capture proceed (which copies the pre-move
position from prevDisplayMetrics) but clear the IsMinimized flag so the
window is restored to its correct position instead of being kept minimized.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

author: mann1x
message:
Fix crash from KeyNotFoundException in SwapWindow and Debug builds

SwapWindow accessed windowProcessName dictionary without ContainsKey
check, causing KeyNotFoundException when window handles become stale.

Also removed #if DEBUG conditional that disabled the try/catch around
WinEventProcCore in Debug builds, causing unhandled exceptions to
terminate the process.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
